segments.s765-g9: «سه روز» is a preposed time expression meaning "for three days." «اندر» is an archaic/poetic preposition meaning "in/within." «آن جایگه» = "that place" (جایگه is a poetic variant of جای). «جنگ بود» is a nominal predicate with بود as the past-tense copula: literally "there was battle." Word order is Time — Locative — Predicate (typical in poetry).
segments.s765-g10: This line strings two genitive noun phrases together as the affected items: «سر آدمی» (heads of men) and «سم اسپان» (the hooves of the horses; اسپان is the plural). «به سود» is a prepositional phrase (به + noun) meaning "to/for profit/advantage" and scopes over the preceding coordinated items. The verb is omitted here and understood from context (a common poetic ellipsis).
segments.s765-g11: «شد» is the past of شدن meaning "became." «ازتشنگی» gives the cause: "from / because of thirst." «دست‌گردان» is a compound adjective meaning "with a wavering/faltering hand" (دست = hand, گردان = turning/wavering) and functions as a predicative description. «ز کار» (ز = poetic from/because of) here means "from the work/affair," i.e. from the fighting; the phrase describes a weakening of the hand because of thirst and battle.
segments.s765-g12: «هم» = "also/too." «اسپ گرانمایه» = "the precious/costly steed" (an adjectival modifier after the noun). «از کارزار» = "from the battlefield" (از = from/of). The verb is omitted but understood from the previous clause (an elliptical use: "also the fine horse was lost/affected in the battle").
segments.s765-g13: «لب رستم» = "Rostam's lip" (possessor follows the noun in Persian). «از تشنگی شد» = "became from thirst" — شد is the copular/verb 'became' and از تشنگی gives the reason. «چو خاک» is a simile: چو = "like/as," خاک = "dust/earth," so "became like dust."
segments.s765-g14: «دهن خشک» = "mouth dry" (adjective after noun). «و» links the phrase to the following description. «گویا» here is conjunctive/parenthetical: "it seems / as if." «زبان چاک چاک» = "tongue (is) torn/split" — the reduplication چاک چاک intensifies the sense of being ripped/cleft. The clause describes the state resulting from thirst.
segments.s765-g15: «چو» = "when." «بریان و گریان شدند» uses two participial/adjectival forms joined by و: بریان (poetic for scorched/consumed/terribly affected) and گریان (weeping); شدند is the past plural of شدن "they became." «از نبرد» marks cause: "from the battle." The clause sets the condition/time for the following action.
segments.s765-g16: «گل‌تر» here uses گل in the sense of "mud/clay" (گل‌تر = more muddy / the mud) as the object. The construction «به خوردن گرفت» is periphrastic: به + verbal noun (خوردن) + گرفت = "began to eat." «اسپ و مرد» (horse and man) are the coordinated subjects/agents of that action. Word order is typical poetic inversion where the verb phrase precedes the coordinated subject.
segments.s765-g17: خروشی is the noun subject 'a roar/cry.' بر آمد is the past verbal phrase (poetic split form of برآمد) meaning 'arose/rose up' (third person plural/singular past form depending on context; here singular). به کردار is a prepositional simile: به + کردار ('in the manner/conduct of') = 'like/in the fashion of.' رعد is the noun 'thunder' completing the simile.
segments.s765-g18: ازین is از + این ('from this/this side') with ین here the orthographic/poetic form of 'این.' روی means 'face/side.' The construction places the side first ('from this side Rostam') and then وزان ('و' + 'زان' = 'and from that') introduces the contrasting side for سعد. The two names (رستم, سعد) are placed after their respective locative phrases.
segments.s765-g19: برفتند is a past plural verb 'they departed/they went away.' هر دو ('each two' = 'both') identifies the two actors. ز قلب سپاه is a genitive/partitive phrase 'from the heart/core of the army' (ز = 'from', قلب = 'core/center', سپاه = 'army'). Word order is typical poetic: verb, subject phrase, then source phrase.
segments.s765-g20: بیکسو is an adverbial phrase meaning 'to one side/aside.' کشیدند is the past plural of کشیدن 'they drew/moved' here used in the sense 'they moved/drew themselves' (i.e. shifted). ز آوردگاه follows as the origin 'from the battlefield.' Together the clause means they moved aside away from the battlefield.
segments.s765-g21: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when.' از لشکر آن is a prepositional phrase 'from that army.' هر دو 'both' identifies the two persons. تنها شدند is the compound past 'they became alone / they were left alone' (شدند = became/were). The clause sets the temporal circumstance for what follows.
segments.s765-g22: به زیر is a directional prepositional phrase 'to/under' (به + زیر = 'underneath'), یکی سرو 'a single cypress' is the locative reference, and بالا شدند ('became high' / 'rose up') is a verbal phrase meaning 'they rose up' or 'they stood up tall.' The poetic order places the locative before the verb.
segments.s765-g23: همی‌تاختند is an archaic/poetic imperfective past form: همی- (continuous/repeated action) + تاختند (they charged) = 'they kept charging' or 'they repeatedly charged.' اندر آوردگاه is a literary locative 'in/within the battlefield.'
segments.s765-g24: دو سالار 'two chiefs/commanders' names the actors; هر دو ('each two' = 'both') repeats/emphasizes that both chiefs share the following state. به دل is 'into/within the heart' (به used directionally), کینه is 'hatred/grudge,' and خواه here is the verbal/adjectival element 'desiring/wishing' (the compound meaning 'harboring hatred' or 'animated by hatred'). Grammatically the phrase modifies the two chiefs: both were driven by hatred in their hearts.
segments.s765-g25: خروشی (a noun) is the subject/thing that 'برآمد' — the verb برآمد is past tense 'arose/issued forth'. ز رستم is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from Rostam'. چو introduces a simile 'like/as' and رعد is the point of comparison 'thunder'. Word order is noun + verb + source + simile.
segments.s765-g26: یکی here functions adverbially as 'one/once' (one stroke). تیغ زد is a light periphrastic past: تیغ (sword) + زد (struck) = 'struck with a sword'; زد is 3rd-person past, subject omitted (understood from context = Rostam). بر سر is the prepositional phrase 'at/on the head (of...)', and اسپ سعد is a genitive 'the horse of Sa'd'.
segments.s765-g27: چو (here fused as چواسپ) is the temporal 'when' introducing the clause. نبرد 'battle' is the place/scene; اندرآمد functions verbally as 'entered/came into' (poetic use). به سر connects to the next clause as the topic 'the head (of the horse)'.
segments.s765-g28: جدا شد is a passive/resultative 'was separated/was torn off' (شد as past auxiliary/become). ازو 'from it/him' marks separation from the body/host. سعد is the proper name (appositive) and پرخاشخر is an epithet 'quarrelsome, aggressive' attached to that name.
segments.s765-g29: بر (here a prepositional/prefix sense) + آهیخت (poetic past; literally 'sighed' or 'uttered a cry') introduces Rostam's immediate action. رستم is the subject; یکی modifies تیغ تیز as 'one single (sharp) sword'. تیغ تیز is the noun phrase 'a sharp sword'.
segments.s765-g30: بدان here functions as 'with that / by that (weapon)'; تا introduces purpose 'so that/in order that' and governs نماید, a present-subjunctive/optative form 'he may show/perform'. به دو رستخیز is the goal/measure — 'into/for a twofold rousing/uprising' (رستخیز used figuratively as a violent upheaval or great stir).
segments.s765-g31: همی‌خواست is an imperfective/habitual past meaning 'was intending / was about to'. از تن is the source phrase 'from the body'. سرش رابرید is a transitive past clause: سرش 'his head' + را (object clitic attached) + برید (severed/cut off) — together 'would cut off his head'.
segments.s765-g32: ز (زِ) = 'from'. گرد سپه here means 'around/from among the host' (گرد used for 'ranks/around'). این مران را is the definite object 'this Maran' (Maran as a proper name) marked by را, and ندید is the negated past 'did not see'. The clause says he failed to see this Maran among the host.
segments.s765-g1: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb meaning ‘he commanded/said’. تا introduces the subordinate clause (here purpose/result: ‘that/so that’). برکشیدند is past tense, 3rd‑person plural (بر + کشیدن) meaning ‘they lifted/drew up’. The direct object نای (the instrument) follows the verb in the compact poetic order: ‘he commanded that they raise the ney.’
segments.s765-g2: سپاه is the subject ‘the army/host’. اندر آمد is a past action (آمد) with اندر indicating movement into/onto the scene (‘came in/advanced’). چو introduces a simile ‘like/as’, and ز جای (ز = from, جای = place) completes the comparison: the army came forth like the sea rising from its place.
segments.s765-g3: برآمد is past tense ‘arose/appeared’. یکی is an indefinite numeral ‘one/a certain’. ابر is the noun ‘cloud’. و simply links clauses ‘and’. برشد is an older/poetic past form meaning ‘rose up/swelled forth’ (بر + شد). خروش is the noun ‘roar/clamor’. The clause describes a cloud appearing and a roar springing up.
segments.s765-g4: همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle here (‘indeed/so’). کر is an adjective ‘deaf’. شد is the past of شدن ‘became’. مردم تیزگوش is the subject phrase ‘the sharp‑eared/attentive people’. Word order is slightly marked for emphasis: predicate (کر شد) comes before the subject, yielding ‘even the keen‑eared people became deaf/stunned (from the noise).’
segments.s765-g5: سنانهای الماس is a genitive/qualifying noun phrase: سنانهای (plural of سنان, ‘spears/spearheads’) modified by الماس ‘diamond’. در introduces the locative phrase. تیره is the adjective ‘dark’, and گرد here functions to indicate the surrounding mass/roundness (‘about/around’), so در تیره گرد conveys ‘amid/in the dark throng/round darkness’ — a compact, poetic locative.
segments.s765-g6: چو introduces a simile ‘like/as’. آتش is the noun ‘fire’. پس here means ‘behind/after’ in a spatial sense. پرده is ‘curtain/veil’, and لاژورد (lapis‑lazuli) follows as a post‑nominal modifier (typical in Persian poetry): ‘a veil of lapis‑lazuli’. The full image: the spearheads shone like fire behind a lapis veil.
segments.s765-g7: همی is an emphatic particle (‘indeed/just/even’). نیزه is the noun ‘spear’. بر is the preposition ‘on/upon/against’. مغفر is a covering or hood (here ‘helmet/covering’), and آبدار is an adjectival epithet meaning ‘lustrous/juicy/rich’ used poetically to describe the sheen of the covering. Together: ‘even the spear upon the gleaming covering…’.
segments.s765-g8: نیامد is the negative past ‘did not come/arrive’ (here ‘did not penetrate/reach’). به is the directional preposition ‘into/toward’. زخم is ‘wound’. اندرون is the locative noun ‘inside/inner parts’. پایدار is an adjective ‘lasting/steady’ modifying زخم. The clause says the spear did not enter to inflict a lasting internal wound.
segments.s765-g49: هزیمت گرفتند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: هزیمت (a rout/defeat) + گرفتند (past plural) yields ‘suffered a rout / were routed.’ The verb گرفتند follows the noun in this poetic order; ایرانیان is the overt subject (postverbal here) meaning ‘the Iranians.’
segments.s765-g50: This clause is a passive/state description: بسی is an intensive/adverbial (poetically ‘many’/‘much’), نامور is an adjective modifying implicit people (‘renowned’), and کشته شد uses کشته (past participle ‘killed’) plus شد as the auxiliary ‘became/were’ to form a passive ‘were killed.’ در میان is a locative phrase ‘among / in the midst.’
segments.s765-g51: بسی is an intensive (‘many’/‘much’) and تشنه is a predicate adjective ‘thirsty’; the phrase بر زین is a locative prepositional phrase ‘on (their) saddle(s).’ بمردند is past plural ‘they died,’ and نیز at the end is the enclitic adverb ‘also/too,’ linking this clause to the previous statement.
segments.s765-g52: پر آمد is a light/compound verbal idea where پر (full/of) + آمد (came) together mean ‘became full’ or ‘was filled.’ ز marks the source/possessor (‘of/from’), here followed by شاهان جهان (the kings of the world), را is the definite object/ topical marker, and قفیز is the noun being described as filled — a rare/obscure lexeme whose exact meaning is lexical but functions syntactically as the object.
segments.s765-g53: سوی شاه ایران is a directional prepositional phrase ‘toward the king of Iran.’ بیامد is the past tense verb ‘came,’ and سپاه is the subject ‘army’ placed after the verb (verb–object/subject inversion common in epic poetry).
segments.s765-g54: This is a compact adverbial phrase describing the march: شب تیره ‘dark night,’ و links coordinate phrases, روز تازان uses تازان as an adjectival/participial sense ‘rushing/ferocious’ describing the days, and به راه is an idiom ‘on the road / on the march.’ The sequence gives a rhythmic description of travel by night and by daring days.
segments.s765-g55: به بغداد here functions locatively/locutionally as ‘in Baghdad’ (poetic inverted order), بود is the past tense copula ‘was,’ آن زمان is a temporal phrase ‘that time / at that time,’ and یزدگرد is the proper name (the subject indicated by the clause).
segments.s765-g56: که is the relative/connecting particle ‘who/that,’ او را uses او (he) plus را (object/topic marker) to mark him as the one affected, سپاه is ‘an army,’ اندآورد is a past verb ‘brought’ and گرد is used adverbially/prepositionally ‘around/about’ — together giving the idea ‘who had an army brought around him’ or ‘about whom an army was gathered.’
segments.s765-g33: فرود آمد is a compound past action: فرود (alighting/fall) + آمد (came) = 'alighted / descended'. The prepositional phrase از پشت زین پلنگ follows the verb as usual and specifies source/place: از 'from', پشت 'the rear/back/behind', زین 'saddle', پلنگ 'leopard' — together 'from the leopard's saddle'.
segments.s765-g34: زد is the simple past 3rd-person 'he struck'. The initial به is a directional/targeting particle ('towards/at'); two repeated بر + noun phrases mark two hit-places in coordination: بر کمر 'upon the waist' and بر سر پالهنگ 'upon the head of the palahang (leopard)'. The subject is omitted because it is carried over from the narrative.
segments.s765-g35: بپوشید is past tense meaning 'was/were covered' or 'covered over' (پوشیدن used causatively/covering here). دیدار رستم is a noun phrase 'the sight/appearance of Rostam' and ز گرد ('by/from dust') gives the cause/material: 'was covered by dust'. Word order places the verb before the postpositional phrase for emphasis, a frequent poetic arrangement.
segments.s765-g36: بشد is a narrative past/casual perfect sense 'it came to be / it happened' introducing the next event. سعد پویان here functions as a proper name (two elements: سعد + پویان). به جای نبرد is a fixed phrase 'to the place of battle' or 'took his place in the fight' (به = to/towards, جای = place, نبرد = battle).
segments.s765-g37: یکی here means 'someone' (an indefinite agent). تیغ زد is a short clause where تیغ 'sword' is the instrument and زد is the past 'struck' (i.e. 'struck with a sword'). بر سر ترگ اوی is the prepositional target 'upon the head (سر) of his ترگ'; اوی is the possessive/pronominal form 'his'. The exact noun ترگ is a concrete noun attached to 'head' (here glossed as 'ragged piece/tuft' in the text).
segments.s765-g38: که introduces a result/explanatory clause ('so that/and as a result'). خون اندر آمد is literal 'blood came in / came forth' (اندر + آمد = 'came within/from'), i.e. 'blood flowed'. ز تارک بروی specifies origin and direction: 'from the تارک to the روی (face)'; in context تارک means 'crown/top of the head' and بروی indicates movement toward the face.
segments.s765-g39: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. دیدار رستم 'Rostam's appearance' is the subject of the subordinate clause; ز خون 'from/because of blood' gives the reason and تیره شد is the verbal predicate 'became dark/blackened' (شد = became). Together: 'When Rostam's appearance became dark from blood...'.
segments.s765-g40: This is a simple main clause: جهانجوی تازی is a noun phrase 'the world-seeking (ambitious) Tazi (Arab)'. بدو is the dative/adverbial 'onto him / against him', and چیره شد is 'became dominant / prevailed' (چیره = dominant/overcoming, شد = became). Together: 'the ambitious Tazi prevailed over him.'
segments.s765-g41: دگر is an adverb here meaning 'then/again' placed before the verb for emphasis. تیغ زد is a light verbal phrase 'struck (with a) sword' where تیغ names the instrument and زد (past of زدن) is the main verb; the agent بربر follows the verb in this inverted, poetic order. گردنش is گردن + enclitic ش = 'his neck', continuing the object/topic into the next phrase.
segments.s765-g42: به خاک اندر افگند is a compact, archaic clause: به + خاک + اندر (into/within the earth) + افگند (threw, past of افکندن). جنگی تنش is a noun phrase 'his warlike body' (جنگی = 'warlike', تن = 'body') with ش = 'his'; the verb افگند applies to that phrase (he cast his warlike body into the dust).
segments.s765-g43: This is a verbless (nominal) poetic clause: سپاه is the topic 'the army'; از دو رویه 'from both faces/sides' modifies it. خودآگاه here means 'conscious/knowing' or 'assured', and the negative sense that follows in the sentence is implied — the copula (بود) is omitted in poetry, so the phrase functions as 'the army, on both sides, (was) not self-assured/ready.'
segments.s765-g44: کسی را is the patient/emphatic phrase 'anyone/no one' marked with را; سوی پهلوان 'toward the champion' is a directional prepositional phrase. راه نه is a negative existential construction (the verb 'is/was' is omitted); together this yields 'there was no one who would go toward the champion' (کسی interpreted negatively because of the surrounding negation).
segments.s765-g45: همی‌جست is an iterative/continuous past ('kept searching' or 'was seeking'); مر is the dative/ethical 'me' or 'for me'; پهلوان را is the definite object 'the champion' (را marks it); سپاه is the subject placed after the verb (inversion common in verse): 'the army kept searching for my champion.'
segments.s765-g46: برفتند is simple past 3rd plural 'they went/advanced.' تا here means 'as far as' (indicating extent), and پیش آوردگاه 'the front (پیش) of the آوردگاه (battlefield/venue)' gives the destination: 'they advanced as far as the front of the battlefield.'
segments.s765-g47: بدیدندش is the past 'they saw him' (دیدن + 3rd-person object enclitic ش). از دور = 'from a distance.' پر خون و خاک is a predicate adjective cluster meaning 'full of blood and dust' (پر = 'full (of)'). The sequence describes his appearance as observed from afar.
segments.s765-g48: سرا پای کردن here is a classical compound meaning 'to rend/sunder from head to foot' (سراپای = whole body); کردن is the light verb 'to make/do' attached to that compound. به شمشیر چاک gives the instrument and result: 'by the sword, (was) gashed/split' — so the whole phrase describes his entire body being cut apart by the sword. (Note: سرا here functions as part of the compound 'سراپای' rather than as 'inn'.)

sentences.s765-s5: For three days the fighting continued in that place; men's heads and the horses' hooves proved to be of profit.
sentences.s765-s6: From thirst his hand grew wavering in the fighting; the costly steed, too, was lost from the battlefield.
sentences.s765-s7: Rostam's lip, from thirst, became like dust; his mouth was dry and his tongue seemed split and torn.
sentences.s765-s8: When they were scorched and weeping from the battle, horse and man began to eat the mud.
sentences.s765-s9: A roar rose like thunder; from this side Rostam, and from that side Saʿd.
sentences.s765-s10: Both of them left the heart of the army and drew aside from the battlefield.
sentences.s765-s11: When both were separated from that army, they rose up beneath a single tall cypress.
sentences.s765-s12: The two chiefs kept charging into the battlefield, both with hatred in their hearts.
sentences.s765-s13: A roar rose from Rostam like thunder; he struck once with his sword at the head of Sa'd's horse.
sentences.s765-s14: When the horse entered the fray, its head was severed from its body — Sa'd the quarrelsome.
sentences.s765-s15: Rostam uttered a cry and raised a single sharp sword, so that with it he might effect a twofold uprising.
sentences.s765-s16: He was about to cut off his head from his body; he did not see this Maran from among the host.
sentences.s765-s1: He ordered them to raise their reeds; the army advanced like a sea from its place.
sentences.s765-s2: A cloud arose and a roar swelled up; even the sharp‑eared people were stunned.
sentences.s765-s3: Diamond spearheads gleamed amid the dark throng, like fire behind a lapis‑lazuli veil.
sentences.s765-s4: A spear struck the gleaming covering, but it did not reach a lasting wound within.
sentences.s765-s25: The Iranians were routed; many famed men were killed among them.
sentences.s765-s26: Many, thirsty, also died on their saddles; the quiver of the kings of the world was filled.
sentences.s765-s27: An army came toward the king of Iran, by dark nights and by days of assault on the road.
sentences.s765-s28: At that time Yazdegerd was in Baghdad, who had an army brought about him.
sentences.s765-s17: He leapt down from the leopard's saddle and struck at the leopard's waist and at its head.
sentences.s765-s18: Rostam's form was shrouded in dust; Saʿd Puyan took up his place in the fight.
sentences.s765-s19: Someone struck with a sword at his ragged head, so that blood ran from his crown down over his face.
sentences.s765-s20: When Rostam's appearance grew dark with blood, the ambitious Tazi overpowered him.
sentences.s765-s21: Then the barbarian struck with his sword and cast his neck to the ground, throwing down his warlike body.
sentences.s765-s22: From both flanks the army had no one who would go toward the champion.
sentences.s765-s23: The host kept searching for my champion; they advanced as far as the front of the battlefield.
sentences.s765-s24: They saw him from afar, all covered with blood and dust, his whole body split by the sword.
